coredata.dc:title: Red emissive fluorescent carbon dots based on ternary carbon source for imaging α-synuclein fibrils
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jintao
Luo, Wan-Chun
Zhang, Yu
Li, Xi
Jiang, Ming
Huang, Kun
Yu, Xu
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: The misfolding and aggregation of α-synuclein monomers usually cause the occurrence and development of Parkinson’s disease (PD). It is important to develop effective methods for detection of α-synuclein aggregates. Carbon dots (CDs) could be the potential fluorescence probe for this purpose owing to their appreciated optical properties. However, undefined structure of CDs and complicated three-dimensional structure of protein severely hindered the design of fluorescence probe towards protein aggregates. Herein, a red emissive fluorescent amphiphilic CD, named as CL-9, was designed with a high sensitivity to α-synuclein fibrils by a one-step heating process, using the ternary carbon source, including Congo red, l-tryptophan and urea. The CL-9 exhibited turn-on red emissive fluorescence towards α-synuclein fibril, but remained no change towards its monomer. Compared with the original Congo red dye, CL-9 exhibited stronger turn-on red fluorescence towards α-synuclein fibrils with better anti-photobleaching resistance, biocompatibility and signal-to-noise ratio. The CL-9 was successful as a fluorescent probe to image α-synuclein fibrils in NL-5901 C. elegans. The present study provided a feasible approach using the multiple carbon sources to construct the CDs based fluorescence probe targeting amyloid proteins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: α-synuclein
Parkinson’s disease
Amyloid protein aggregation
Carbon dots
Congo red
Fluorescence probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CL-CDs towards α-syn-F in vitro
Investigation of the fluorescence property of CL-CDs in different microenvironments
In vivo imaging α-syn aggregates in transgenic elegans by CL-CDs
Response of CL-9 to α-syn-F in vitro
The mechanism of fluorescence response of CL-9 towards amyloid fibrils
Imaging α-syn-F in NL5901 C. elegans by CL-9 in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support of this work by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82373834). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST and the Medical Subcenter of HUST Analytical & Testing Center for material characterization and data acquisition.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CL-9. (A) TEM image of the CL-9. (B) The particle size distribution for CL-9. (C) FTIR spectra of CL-9, CR, l-Trp and urea. (D) Full-survey scanning XPS spectrum of CL-9. High-resolution XPS spectra of (E) C 1s, (F) N 1s, (G) O 1s and (H) S 2p of CL-9.

(A) The fluorescence responses of CL-9 (5 μg/mL) towards different amyloid fibrils (5 μM). (B) The fluorescence spectra of CL-9 (5 μg/mL) in the presence of α-syn-F or α-syn-M (5 μM). (C) The excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of CL-9 (5 μg/mL) in the presence of α-syn-F (5 μM). (D) Fluorescence response of CL-9 (5 μg/mL) towards α-syn-F of varying concentrations (0.5–10 μM). (E) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CL-9 and the concentration of α-syn-F (0.5–10 μM). (F) The response of CL-9 (5 μg/mL) to α-syn-F and various potential interferences (5 μM), including blank (CT), metal ions 1–8 (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+ in the order), amino acid 9–14 (d-Trp, l-Trp, l-Phe, l-Cys, l-Met, l-Arg and l-His in the order), and α-syn-F.

Fluorescence spectra of CL-9 in (A) water-glycerol binary solvents system simulating different viscosities and (B) different solvents representing different polarities. The binding mode of l-Trp (C) or CR (D) with α-syn-F by molecular docking studies.
Fluorescence images of NL-5901 C. elegans labeled by CL-9.

head.ce:title: Red emissive fluorescent carbon dots based on ternary carbon source for imaging α-synuclein fibrils

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The misfolding and aggregation of α-synuclein monomers usually cause the occurrence and development of Parkinson’s disease (PD). It is important to develop effective methods for detection of α-synuclein aggregates. Carbon dots (CDs) could be the potential fluorescence probe for this purpose owing to their appreciated optical properties. However, undefined structure of CDs and complicated three-dimensional structure of protein severely hindered the design of fluorescence probe towards protein aggregates. Herein, a red emissive fluorescent amphiphilic CD, named as CL-9, was designed with a high sensitivity to α-synuclein fibrils by a one-step heating process, using the ternary carbon source, including Congo red, l-tryptophan and urea. The CL-9 exhibited turn-on red emissive fluorescence towards α-synuclein fibril, but remained no change towards its monomer. Compared with the original Congo red dye, CL-9 exhibited stronger turn-on red fluorescence towards α-synuclein fibrils with better anti-photobleaching resistance, biocompatibility and signal-to-noise ratio. The CL-9 was successful as a fluorescent probe to image α-synuclein fibrils in NL-5901 C. elegans. The present study provided a feasible approach using the multiple carbon sources to construct the CDs based fluorescence probe targeting amyloid proteins.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The process of aberrant protein aggregation is a complicated multistep, and often results in neurodegenerative diseases, metabolic abnormalities and certain malignancies, including liver cancer, diabetes mellitus type II, Parkinson's disease (PD) and Alzheimer's disease (AD) [1–4]. Decades of studies have disclosed that protein misfolding generates misfolded monomers, which subsequently combine to produce soluble oligomers and finally aggregate into insoluble amyloid fibrils [5,6]. Protein aggregates are characterized as deposits in the human specific tissues or organs, which are recognized as the significant biomarkers for clinical diagnosis [7–9]. Therefore, it is meaningful to detect amyloid aggregates in order to realize the early diagnosis and intervene diseases for clinic purpose, and gain a thorough grasp of the mechanism underlying protein aggregation in fundamental researches.

PD is a common neurodegenerative disease that usually starts slowly and worsens with time, exerting a serious negative impact on the life quality of the patient [10]. The occurrence and development of PD are highly related to the abnormal aggregation of α-synuclein (α-syn) [11,12]. Generally, α-syn transits from α-helical state to β-sheet-rich state with pathological aggregation, which leads to the deposition of α-syn fibrils (α-syn-F) in the brain regions [13]. Hence, one characteristics of PD is the deposition of α-syn-F, which is claimed to be an important biomarker for PD [14]. Thus, it is of great significance to establish the proper method to detect α-syn-F for the drug development, pathological studies, and intervention of the disease.

Fluorescence imaging technique, characterized with non-invasiveness, visualization, convenience and low cost, is promising for amyloids detection [15,16]. As a family of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attentions for this purpose due to their excellent fluorescence property, good biocompatibility, negligible cytotoxicity, ease of synthesis and low cost [17–19]. For example, Wang et al. proposed a human serum albumin functionalized CDs (HSA-BFP@CDs) to realize the multi-functions to image β-amyloid fibrils (Aβ-F), inhibit Aβ aggregation and scavenge free radicals [20]. However, the signal-to-noise ratio of this HSA-BFP@CDs probe was slightly limited. Also, our group found that Congo red (CR) could be easily carbonized to generate fluorescent CDs, i.e. EDA-CDs, which reserved the turn-on red fluorescence response of CR towards protein aggregates [21]. Nevertheless, the EDA-CDs still suffered from relatively low sensitivity and limitations for in vivo imaging. Anyway, most of the published studies about the fluorescent CDs probe for amyloids focused on Aβ-F, but seldom on α-syn-F. To develop efficient imaging probes for amyloids, especially α-syn-F, we are making our effort to construct the CDs with a high signal-to-noise ratio.

However, the design of CDs for specific target is still challenging because of undefined structure of CDs and complicated three-dimensional structure of protein [22,23]. Fortunately, the properties of CDs could be controllably regulated via the fine selection of carbon sources, as the properties of carbon sources could be reserved to some degree in CDs [24–26]. In our previous work, it is disclosed that CR derived CDs (EDA-CDs) can specifically identify the β-sheet structure of protein aggregates since the residual functional groups of CR in the CDs could play the dual role as recognition and responsive element [21]; thus, CR was a crucial carbon source to generate this EDA-CDs probe for imaging amyloids. In light of CR as one necessary carbon source, we attempted to adopt other carbon sources to enhance the sensing performance of CR-derived CDs towards protein aggregates. Herein, l-tryptophan (l-Trp), a hydrophobic aromatic amino acid, was particularly noticed. Firstly, the hydrophobicity of l-Trp was supposed to facilitate the hydrophobic interaction between probes and protein aggregates. Secondly, the amino and carboxyl groups of l-Trp would offer extra hydrogen bond interactions between CDs and amino acid residues in the fibrils. More importantly, l-Trp was reported to target β-sheet structure of protein aggregates [27–29]; thus, it may have synergistic effect with CR to target protein aggregates. All of these properties could assist the recognition and response of the CDs probe towards protein aggregates, promoting the specificity and sensitivity of the probe. Based on these considerations, l-Trp was adopted as the other carbon source.

However, hydrophilicity of the probe is also essential to in vivo applications [30]. Thus, in addition to the above considerations about hydrophobic and hydrogen bond interactions, hydrophilicity of the probe should be considered. To make a balance between hydrophobicity and hydrophilicity of the probe, amphiphilic CDs attracted our attention. Amphipathy of CDs could guarantee the dispersibility of the probe in aqueous biological environment to avoid adding co-solvents (such as dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and ethanol (EtOH)) and precipitating. Meanwhile, it may also ensure the hydrophobic interactions with protein aggregates in a hydrophilic environment. To construct the amphiphilic CDs, urea, a good soluble substance with abundant nitrogen, was adopted as another carbon source, aiming at improving hydrophilicity of the probe. The rich amino-groups of urea may also offer extra hydrogen bond interactions between CDs and amino acid residues in the fibrils. Therefore, l-Trp, urea, and CR were used as ternary carbon sources to construct the amphiphilic CDs, named as CL-CDs. Thus-obtained CDs exerted turn-on red fluorescence towards amyloid fibrils with high sensitivity.

Details regarding the apparatus and reagents used in this study can be found in the Supporting Information. The procedures to prepare insulin fibrils (ins-F), lysozyme fibrils (lys-F), Aβ-F and α-syn-F were presented in Supporting information (S2).

CL-CDs were synthesized according to the method reported by our group with minor modification [21]. Generally, urea, CR and l-Trp of certain amount were dissolved in 30.0 mL ultrapure water, and then transferred into a 100 mL flask with vigorous stirring under refluxing at fixed temperature for prescribed time. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the obtained primary products were filtered with a Millipore filter (0.22 µm) to remove large particles and further dialyzed against deionized water with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24.0 h. Finally, the CL-CDs were obtained by evaporating the water inside the dialysis bag at 70.0 °C.

To obtain the best sensing performance of CL-CDs towards α-syn-F, the reactant doping amount ratio, reaction temperature and reaction time were explored. For clarity, the obtained products and their corresponding synthetic conditions are listed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CL-CDs towards α-syn-F in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of CL-CDs towards different amyloid fibrils was investigated, including ins-F, lys-F, Aβ-F and α-syn-F. Specially, the response of CL-CDs towards α-syn-monomer (α-syn-M) was also explored. Typically, 10 μL of amyloid fibrils or monomers (100 µM) and 1 μL CL-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) were sequentially added into 189 μL PB buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4). The fluorescence spectra were scanned.

The selectivity of CL-CDs towards α-syn-F was explored by adding 1 μL CL-CDs (1 mg/mL) into respective PB buffer containing 5 μM of each investigated interfering species, including Na+, K+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, d-tryptophan (d-Trp), l-tryptophan (l-Trp), l-phenylalanine (l-Phe), l-cysteine (l-Cys), l-methionine (l-Met), l-arginine (l-Arg) and l-histidine (l-His). The fluorescence intensities of these samples were measured (λex = 512 nm, λem = 616 nm).

The detection linearity of CL-CDs towards the α-syn-F dispersion was carried out in the range of 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5 and 10 μg/mL (λex = 512 nm, λem = 601 nm). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on 3σ/k (σ was the standard deviation of blank sample, and k was the slope of the curve).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the fluorescence property of CL-CDs in different microenvironments
ce:section.ce:para: The microenvironmental conditions, e.g. polarity, viscosity and pH, could influence the fluorescence property of the probe. To explore the fluorescence response of CL-CDs to polarity, 1 μL CL-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) was added into 199 μL of the individual solvents, including dichloromethane (DCM), 1,4-dioxane, tetrahydrofuran (THF), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), DMSO, EtOH, methanol (MeOH) and water, with incubation for 30 min. The fluorescence spectra were then collected.

The fluorescence response of CL-CDs towards viscosity was investigated in the binary solvents of glycerol and water with varied volume ratios (0 %, 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, 100 %), which simulated varied microenvironmental viscosities. Specifically, 1 μL CL-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) was dispersed in 199 μL of glycerol-water binary solvents with incubation for 30 min, and then the fluorescence spectra were collected.

The fluorescence response of CL-CDs towards pH was also studied by dispersing 1 μL CL-CDs (1 mg/mL) in 199 μL PB (10 mM) with pH values ranging from 4.86 to 8.00, and then subjecting to the fluorescence intensity measurement (λex = 512 nm, λem = 616 nm).

The AutoDock 4.2.6 plugin was used to carry out the molecular docking studies, and PyMol software (Version 2.3, Schrödinger) was used to visualize the binding mode of CR or l-Trp with α-syn-F (PDB ID: 2N0A) [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging α-syn aggregates in transgenic elegans by CL-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the imaging ability of CL-CDs towards α-syn aggregates in vivo, the Transgenic Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) strain NL5901 were used as the studied model, the non-transgenic Caenorhabditis elegans N2 (wild type, WT) were treated as the negative control. Typically, C. elegans NL5901 or N2 nematode was cultured on OP50/NGM solid medium at a constant temperature of 20 °C until the L4 adult stage. The NL5901 or N2 worms of the L4 stage were rinsed with the M9 solution (containing 22 mM KH2PO4, 42.3 mM Na2HPO4, 85.5 mM NaCl and 1 mM MgSO4), and centrifuged at 2,000 rpm for 1 min. The precipitated NL5901 or N2 nematodes were collected and washed three times with the M9 solution. Next, 10 μg/mL CL-CDs were incubated with the NL5901 or N2 nematodes for 30 min, respectively. Finally, the labeled nematodes were washed again with the M9 solution for three times and anesthetized with levamisole (2 mM in the M9 solution) on a 3 % agarose pad for fluorescence microscopic observation.

It is known that CR is a kind of azo dye and responds to amyloids fibrils with turn-on red fluorescence. However, it is unsuitable for in vivo applications because of its toxicity and possible negative effects on aquatic animals and human health [32]. In our previous study, CR-derived CDs, i. e. EDA-CDs, were designed with lowered toxicity but inheriting the turn-on red fluorescence response of CR towards protein aggregates [21]. However, the optimal signal-to-noise ratio of EDA-CDs towards Ins-F (5 μM) was unsatisfactory and only 2.0, which severely restricted in vivo applicability of this kind of CDs. Anyway, in that study, we found that nano-derivatization of CR to be CDs was effective to reduce the toxicity while reserve the properties of CR as both the recognition and responsive element [21]. It may be generalized that CDs based fluorescence probe could be designed on purpose for specific target by adjusting carbon sources with improved desirable performance. With our continuous effort to analyze amyloids, herein, we designed de novo another CR-derived CDs, i.e. CL-CDs, utilizing CR as the recognition and responsive element but further enhancing the detection sensitivity.

To achieve this goal, we particularly noticed interactions between the probe and protein aggregates. It was reported that hydrogen bond or electrostatic interactions could exist between the probe and amino acid residues in the fibrils [19]. From this point, the amino, carboxyl, or hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs would be beneficial for the formation of hydrogen bonds between probe and amino acid residues. Alternatively, hydrophobic interaction between probe and hydrophobic β-sheet of amyloid fibrils could contribute to the response as well. As reported, aromatic amino acids residues participated in amyloids self-assembly processes to form amyloid aggregates, such as tryptophan, phenylalanine and tyrosine [33–35]. These aromatic amino acids were demonstrated as the recognition motif of hydrophobic β-sheet of amyloid fibrils when some small peptides exerted inhibition on protein aggregation [27–29]. Besides, enhanced hydrophobicity of probe may strengthen π-π stacking between probe and hydrophobic aromatic amino acid residues of fibrils [27,36,37]. Thus, it is envisioned that enhancing hydrophobic interaction or hydrogen bond could be a benefit to the probing sensitivity towards protein aggregates. Therefore, l-Trp, a hydrophobic aromatic amino acid, was adopted as a carbon source to improve the hydrophobicity of the CDs, which was expected to strengthen the hydrophobic interaction between the generated CDs and hydrophobic β-sheet of amyloid fibrils, in addition to its targeting specificity towards β-sheet structure [27]. Besides, the amino and carboxyl groups of l-Trp may be reserved in CL-CDs, which would add extra hydrogen bond interaction sites between CDs and amino acid residues in the fibrils. From these respects, l-Trp may assist CR to exert the recognition role towards the amyloid fibrils. Specially, l-Trp, an essential amino acid and a precursor of 5-hydroxytryptamine, could endow the resulting CL-CDs with good biocompatibility to reduce the toxicity of CR.

Apart from the hydrophobicity of the probe, the hydrophilicity should be also paid attention to, considering the in vivo applicability of the probe. Besides, the cost of preparation should be also considered. Thus, urea, a good soluble and economic substance, was used as another carbon source. Meanwhile, its abundant amino groups may be favourable to form extra hydrogen bond interactions between CDs and amino acid residues in the fibrils. Based on these considerations, the ternary carbon source, i.e. l-Trp, urea and CR, was employed to generate the amphiphilic CL-CDs.

To obtain the best turn-on fluorescence response of CL-CDs towards α-syn-F, the amount ratio of l-Trp to CR (l-Trp/CR) was firstly explored. As listed in Table S1, when l-Trp/CR was changed from 0 to 4.00 (CL-(1–4)), the sensing response towards α-syn-F was sharply enhanced and doubled from CL-1 (F/F0 = 5.70) to CL-2 (F/F0 = 10.2), but then remained almost constant (CL-(2–4)). The water contact angles of CL-1, CL-2, CL-3 and CL-4 were measured as 50.8°, 88.3°, 85.7° and 92.8° (Fig. S7), respectively, indicating that the hydrophobicity of CL-(2–4) upon the addition of l-Trp was remarkably stronger than that of CL-1 (without l-Trp), but remained comparably constant irrespective of the varied amount of l-Trp. From this observation, the enhanced sensing performance of CL-2 may be ascribed to the presence of l-Trp in CL-CDs, which would enhance the hydrophobicity of CDs. Therefore, l-Trp/CR of 1.00 was fixed to investigate the effect of urea mass on the sensing performance (CL-5, 6, 2, 7). As can be seen in Table S1, when the urea mass was increased from 0 to 0.5000 g, the sensing performance of CL-5, CL-6 and CL-2 towards α-syn-F was correspondently enhanced, with F/F0 of 5.31, 6.47 and 10.2 in that order. But the further enhancing urea exhibited nearly no influence on the sensing response, CL-7 with F/F0 of 10.1 in this case. As photographed in Fig. S7, the water contact angles of CL-5, CL-6, CL-2 and CL-7 were 94.9°, 92.5°, 88.3° and 86.3°, respectively, indicating that the hydrophilicity of CL-CDs was improved with the increasing urea. Furthermore, the effect of reaction time (CL-8, 2, 9, 10, 11) and temperature (CL-9, 12, 13) on the sensing performance of CL-CDs towards α-syn-F was also investigated, and the biggest F/F0 of 14.9 occurred at 8.0 h and 180 °C. The prolonging reaction time and elevated temperature could facilitate the carbonization of carbon sources. The appropriate carbonization may be required for the optimal sensing response of the probe.

Based on the above experiments, the optimal synthetic conditions of CL-CDs were determined. 0.0500 g CR, 0.0500 g l-Trp and 0.5000 g urea were dissolved in 30.0 mL deionized water, and reacted at 180 °C for 8.0 h. Thus-obtained CL-9 was used for the following studies.

CL-9 was fully characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS), UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1 A), the CL-9 were observed to be well-dispersed quasi spheres with an average particle size of 8.3 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 1B). This observation agreed well with that of AFM (Fig. S8). By referring to the FTIR spectra of CR, l-Trp and urea in Fig. 1C, we found that CL-9 shared almost the same typical functional groups with CR (e.g. NH (3411 cm−1), CH (2916 cm−1), CC (1606 cm−1), CN (1166 cm−1), SO/CO (1112 cm−1 and 1052 cm−1) and CS (626 cm−1)), l-Trp (e.g. NH/OH (3411 cm−1), OH (3402 cm−1), CO (1637 cm−1), CC (1166 cm−1) and CO (1052 cm−1)), and urea (e.g. NH (3411 cm−1), CO (1637 cm−1) and CN (1166 cm−1)), respectively, indicating that CL-9 reserved these functional groups from the ternary carbon sources. The NH, OH and O-CO groups may endow the CL-9 amphiphilicity, as reflected in Fig. S9, the isoelectric point (pI) of CL-9 was determined to be 3.6. This amphiphilicity may enhance the applicability of CL-9, as these hydrophobic and hydrophilic groups would facilitate interactions between CL-9 and amyloid aggregates as discussed in Section 3.1, while the hydrophilicity would enhance the biocompatibility of the probe and make the in vivo application feasible.

In addition to FTIR, the full XPS spectrum disclosed signals of C 1s (284.7 eV), N 1s (399.08 eV), O 1s (531.08 eV) and S 2p (167.08 eV) with corresponding atomic percentages of 69.58 %, 11.05 %, 15.41 % and 3.96 % (Fig. 1D). Specially, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) displayed signals of CC/CC (284.7 eV), CO/CN/CS (285.1–285.8 eV) [38]. Additionally, the CO/O-CO group (287.8––288.9 eV) originating from urea or l-Trp was also detected [39]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 399.3 eV, 400.1 eV and 401.1 eV, attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 1G) [40]. Besides, two divided peaks at 530.4 eV and 531.4 eV for the high-resolution O 1s spectrum were observed, and belonged to CO and SO/SO/CO groups, respectively (Fig. 1H). The high-resolution S 2p spectrum displayed the presence of −SO3 (168.7 eV) and −SO4 (169.9 eV) (Fig. 1G) [41]. Based on the results from XPS, the CL-9 largely reserved the functional groups from the ternary carbon source, e. g. carboxyl groups of l-Trp, amino groups of urea, and sulfonic acid groups of CR, which was consistent with the results of FTIR.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were investigated to explore the optical properties of CL-9. As depicted in Fig. S10A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CL-9 exhibited two absorption peaks at 227 nm and 283 nm, which were ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bond. Besides, two broad absorption bands in the 310–380 nm and 410–590 nm region were also observed with the maximum absorbance at 495 nm, which could be ascertained as the n–π* transition of CO [41,42]. CL-9 was weak fluorescent in water with the
λ
em
max
of 616 nm upon the excitation (
λ
ex
max
) at 512 nm. The quantum yield of CL-9 was calculated as 0.5 ‰ in water with reference to Rhodamine B, further illustrating that CL-9 had a low fluorescence background noise in water which would be beneficial to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio. In addition, CL-9 remained stable fluorescence across a pH range of 4.86–8.00 (Fig. S10B), demonstrating its good pH stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CL-9 to α-syn-F in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Accumulation of misfolded and aggregated proteins causes the occurrence and development of neurodegenerative or metabolic diseases, etc [43]. It is important to detect amyloid fibrils for fundamental research, diagnosis and therapy. In our previous study, we found that CR exerted different responses towards amyloid fibrils, with strengthened turn-on fluorescence in the order of Aβ-F, Lys-F, α-syn-F and Ins-F [21]. Herein, the fluorescence responses of CL-9 towards different amyloid fibrils were investigated (Fig. 2 A). Obviously, the extents of turn-on fluorescence of CL-9 towards amyloid fibrils were different from CR, and strengthened in the order of Aβ-F, Ins-F, Lys-F and α-syn-F, implying that l-Trp may affect the fluorescence response behavior of CL-9 towards amyloid fibrils. As CL-9 exerted the strongest response towards α-syn-F, the α-syn-F was selected as the major target to conduct the further experiments. Besides, the fluorescence responsive signal of CL-9 towards α-syn-F reached plateau at 5 min and remained almost stable within 60 min (Fig. S11). To ensure the detection repeatability, 30 min of incubation time was selected for the following experiments.

At the optimal probing condition, the fluorescence emission of CL-9 remained almost unchanged upon the interaction with α-syn-M (Fig. 2B). By contrast, a 14.9-fold fluorescence enhancement was obtained once CL-9 interacted with the 5 μM α-syn-F (named as α-syn-F + CL-9). Moreover, the maximum emission of the CL-9 (λem = 601 nm, λex = 512 nm, Fig. 2C) exhibited a 15 nm blueshift in the presence of α-syn-F compared to the pure CL-9 (λem = 616 nm, λex = 512 nm, Fig. S10A), which might be caused by the binding of CL-9 towards the hydrophobic β-sheet of α-syn-F [44]. The turn-on fluorescence strengthened with the elevated concentration of α-syn-F (Fig. 2D), and a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity (λem = 601 nm, λex = 512 nm) and the concentration of α-syn-F (0.5–10 μM) was obtained with an LOD of 59 nM (Fig. 2E).

Considering the complexity of coexisting species in the cell microenvironment, the response of CL-9 to potential interferences was also investigated, such as Na+, K+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, d-Trp, l-Trp l-Phe, l-Cys, l-Met, l-Arg and l-His. As depicted in Fig. 2F, only α-syn-F significantly turned on the fluorescence of CL-9. The investigated potential interferences had no obvious effect on the fluorescence response of CL-9 to α-syn-F, indicating that the probe had high sensing selectivity for α-syn-F.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of fluorescence response of CL-9 towards amyloid fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the possible sensing mechanism of CL-9 towards amyloid fibrils, the fluorescence lifetime of CL-9 in the absence and presence of α-syn-F was firstly measured. As shown in Fig. S12, the fluorescence decay curves of CL-9 in the absence and presence of α-syn-F both fitted well with the single exponential decay kinetics, and the lifetime of CL-9 and CL-9+α-syn-F showed a little change, calculated as 1.34 ns and 0.94 ns, respectively. The result indicated that the tested fibrils had a little change on the lifetime of excited state of CL-9, implying the existence of interactions between CL-9 and the protein aggregates [45,46]. Besides, the hydrophobic residues of protein aggregates would be exposed in the process of abnormal protein aggregation, leading to the change of microenvironment, like decreased local polarity and elevated local viscosity [47]. By this principle, many chemical sensors aiming at amyloid fibrils were designed with the aid of microenvironment-sensitive fluorophores [48]. CR, sensitive to viscosity, is a classical dye for imaging amyloid fibrils. Based on the characterizations in Section 3.2, most functional groups of CR were reserved in CL-9. Reasonably, it is speculated that CL-9 may also reserve the viscosity response of CR. Thus, the fluorescence response of CL-9 towards polarity and viscosity was herein investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of CL-9 strengthened in water-glycerol binary solvents with the increasing glycerol ratio (elevated viscosity). Besides, CL-9 showed a blueshift of λem from 616 nm to 601 nm, but λex of CL-9 remained almost unchanged, which agreed well with the observation for CL-9 interacting with α-syn-F (Fig. 2C). Moreover, water and seven organic solvents were chosen to explore the effect of polarity on the fluorescence response of CL-9. As depicted in Fig. 3B, among the studied solvents, the fluorescence intensity of CL-9 was apparently the strongest in DCM (Fig. 3B), which was normally used to mimic the hydrophobic microenvironment of the β-sheet structure of amyloid fibrils [49]. Particularly, the fluorescence intensity of CL-9 was calculated to be about 41-fold stronger in the DCM than that in the water, suggesting that CL-9 may be capable to turn on the fluorescence when interacting with the α-syn-F. In summary, CL-9 could bind to fibrils and respond to the microenvironmental viscosity and polarity, which could be the reasons for the response of CL-9 to the fibrils.

The binding affinity of CL-9 or CR with α-syn-F was further studied. As shown in Fig. S13, the K d values of CL-9 and CR with α-syn-F were measured as 2.417 μg/mL and 5.955 μg/mL, respectively. Obviously, the CL-9 possessed slightly stronger binding affinity with α-syn-F, suggesting that the addition of l-Trp to form CDs may promote the affinity to the fibrils, possibly due to more availability of binding sites profiting from aromatic interactions between the probe and α-syn-F, as discussed in Section 3.1. The binding mode of l-Trp or CR with α-syn-F was further evaluated by molecular docking studies, which could identify the possible binding sites of the probe with protein aggregates. It has been reported that the binding sites in the tunnel along the fibril axis was relatively inaccessible for binding [31]; thus, we assumed one kind of possible conformations on the groove at the surface of fibril axis as predicted binding sites (Fig. 3C and 3D). We docked nine times to find the possible binding mode of l-Trp or CR with α-syn-F. As pictured in Fig. 3C, l-Trp located on the groove at the surface of fibril axis, adjacent to Ser42 and Thr44 residues, with −5.4 kcal/mol docking score. Besides, CR was adjacent to Tyr39 and Thr44 residues, with −11.3 kcal/mol docking score (Fig. 3D). Apparently, in addition to the Tyr39 and Thr44 residues present for CR, l-Trp can offer extra hydrogen bond (Ser42) with amino acid residues in the fibrils, further indicating the synergistic effect of l-Trp with CR to respond to protein aggregates. Therefore, the addition of l-Trp into CDs could be a benefit to promote binding to fibrils, which is consistent with the above binding affinity assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging α-syn-F in NL5901 C. elegans by CL-9 in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The property of photobleaching resistance of CL-9 is key to obtain stable imaging signals in vivo. Thus, the photobleaching resistance of CL-9 was explored. As shown in Fig. S14, both CL-9 and CR exhibited turn-on red fluorescence upon the interaction with the α-syn-F. When exposed to the continuous irradiation, the red fluorescence of the α-syn-F labeled with CR was sharply declined after 5 min irradiation, and almost faded in 10 min. However, for the α-syn-F stained by CL-9, the fluorescence intensity maintained almost constant for 10 min irradiation and even lasted for at least 20 min. This observation demonstrates the superior photobleaching resistance of CL-9 than CR.

Additionally, the cytotoxicity of CL-9 was evaluated using the CCK8 assay in Human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells. As depicted in Fig. S15, the CL-9 (0–200 μg/mL) had low cytotoxicity, indicating its safety for in vivo imaging of α-syn-F.

Based on the above positive imaging results of CL-9 on α-syn-F in vitro, and improved photobleaching resistance and lowered cytotoxicity, we further investigated imaging capabilities of CL-9 in vivo. The typical PD model NL5901 C. elegans expressing human α-syn aggregates tagged with yellow fluorescent protein (α-syn-YFP) in muscle cells were adopted as the studied model, which can report the formation of α-syn aggregates [50,51], and the C. elegans N2 (WT type) without expressing α-syn-F were treated as the negative control. As shown in Fig. S16, the fluorescent signals of green or red channel were almost dark in the head and body region of C. elegans N2, which means that C. elegans N2 had no influence on the fluorescence of CL-9. As a contrast, the green fluorescence of α-syn aggregates derived from the labeled YFP was observed at both the head and body region in NL5901 nematode (Fig. 4 ), which merged well with the red fluorescence originating from the CL-9. Besides, the Pearson’s correlation coefficient of CL-9 with α-syn-YFP was calculated as 0.85 (Fig. S17), demonstrating the excellent imaging ability of our CL-9 for α-syn aggregates in vivo.

In this study, we designed de novo a CR-derived CDs with a high sensitivity towards α-syn aggregates. For this CR-derived CDs, the ternary carbon source was adopted, i.e. CR, l-Trp and urea, resulting in an amphiphilic CD (CL-9). The l-Trp played the roles to enhance the affinity with fibrils, collaborate with CR for recognition of the target and improve the biocompatibility. Urea was used to improve hydrophilicity of the probe, and its abundant amino groups may promote extra hydrogen bond interactions between the probe and amino acid residues in the fibrils. The red fluorescence of CL-9 could be turned on upon the interaction with amyloid fibrils. Especially for α-syn-F, the fluorescence enhancement could reach about 14.9-fold. This specificity of CL-9 towards α-syn-F differed from CR, which could be largely due to the role of l-Trp. With satisfactory turn-on red fluorescence response, good biocompatibility and enhanced photobleaching resistance, CL-9 succeeded in imaging α-syn fibrils in NL5901 C. elegans. The present construction method provided a feasible approach to design the applicable CDs based fluorescence probe for amyloid aggregates.

Jintao Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Wan-Chun Luo: Methodology. Yu Zhang: Methodology. Xi Li: Methodology. Ming Jiang: Writing – review & editing. Kun Huang: Supervision, Project administration. Xu Yu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Li Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support of this work by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82373834). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST and the Medical Subcenter of HUST Analytical & Testing Center for material characterization and data acquisition.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.05.129.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
